claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 10 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 10 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

**First, let's talk about your child (or children):**

How old is the child you're claiming for? The age determines which eligibility requirements apply:

- **Under 16**: Generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- **16 to 19**: May qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible

Could you please tell me:
1. How many children are you looking to claim Child Benefit for?
2. What are their ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my two children - Alex who is 5 years old, and Blake who is 10. They both live with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me work through the eligibility for both Alex (5 years old) and Blake (10 years old).

Since both children are under 16, they fall into the category that generally qualifies automatically. Let me check the next requirement for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, for children under 16, I need to verify a few conditions:

**Are either Alex or Blake working 24 or more hours per week in paid employment?**

(This is typically not the case for children aged 5 and 10, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10 - they're both still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?**

(Again, this is unlikely for children their age, but I need to confirm for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just kids - I'm the one who handles all that sort of thing for the family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming that. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake enrolled in any type of apprenticeship program?**

(This is another formality for children aged 5 and 10, but I need to check this requirement.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. They're way too young for that - they're still in primary school. Alex is only 5 and Blake is 10!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes complete sense. Now let me move on to questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status, as this is a key requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet any of the following residency requirements?**

1. **Do you normally live in the UK?** (This is the most common qualifying path)

OR any of these special circumstances:

2. Have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?
3. Have pre-settled status and are working/studying/job seeking?
4. Are you living abroad as a Crown servant?

The easiest path is if you simply live in the UK normally. **Do you normally reside in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. We're all based here - me, Alex, and Blake. This is our home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That satisfies the residency requirement. Now let me check the next important criterion.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to confirm responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You mentioned earlier that they both live with you at home. This means you meet the "living with the child" requirement, which is one of the main ways to show responsibility.

Just to confirm: **Do Alex and Blake both live with you in your home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Both Alex and Blake live with me at home. I look after them - they're my kids and we all live together.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That clearly establishes your responsibility for both children. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check an important question about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them, and nobody else has made a claim.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's clear. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check the children's living situation to make sure there are no special circumstances that might affect eligibility.

**Are either Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like they're both living normally at home with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. They're both just living at home with me - nothing special or unusual. They're not in hospital, care, or anything like that. Just normal family life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

This would be things like the council paying for their housing or upkeep. Based on what you've told me about your normal family situation, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I'm the one providing for them - they're my children living in my home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's all the information I need. Let me now provide you with the eligibility outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE for both Alex and Blake**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for both of your children. Here's why:

### For Alex (age 5):
✅ Under 16 years old  
✅ Not in paid employment  
✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right  
✅ You live in the UK (residency requirement met)  
✅ You have responsibility for Alex (living with you)  
✅ No one else is claiming for Alex  
✅ Living normally at home with you  
✅ Council not paying towards their care  

### For Blake (age 10):
✅ Under 16 years old  
✅ Not in paid employment  
✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right  
✅ You live in the UK (residency requirement met)  
✅ You have responsibility for Blake (living with you)  
✅ No one else is claiming for Blake  
✅ Living normally at home with you  
✅ Council not paying towards their care  

---

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and each child
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake
4. **Payments** will be made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

## Important Note - High Income Child Benefit Charge:
⚠️ If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge through a tax adjustment. You can still claim, but some or all of it may need to be repaid via your tax return.

## More Information:
You can find full details on how to claim here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Congratulations, and best wishes to you and your family! 🏠👨‍👩‍👧‍👦

reasonings: Alex (age 5) meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies in the qualifying child category; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in the UK); (6) Claimant has responsibility for the child (lives with the child); (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for this child; (8) Child is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (9) Local council is not paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 10) meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies in the qualifying child category; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in the UK); (6) Claimant has responsibility for the child (lives with the child); (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for this child; (8) Child is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (9) Local council is not paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children (Alex, age 5, and Blake, age 10). Both children are under 16 and living normally at home with the claimant who has full responsibility for them. The claimant meets UK residency requirements, and there are no disqualifying factors such as other claimants, council payments, or special living arrangements. All eligibility criteria have been satisfied for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 5) meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies in the qualifying child category; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in the UK); (6) Claimant has responsibility for the child (lives with the child); (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for this child; (8) Child is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (9) Local council is not paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 10) meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies in the qualifying child category; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in the UK); (6) Claimant has responsibility for the child (lives with the child); (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for this child; (8) Child is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered); (9) Local council is not paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children (Alex, age 5, and Blake, age 10). Both children are under 16 and living normally at home with the claimant who has full responsibility for them. The claimant meets UK residency requirements, and there are no disqualifying factors such as other claimants, council payments, or special living arrangements. All eligibility criteria have been satisfied for both children.
